samples.title: Fitness club closure — email to a friend

task: You are a member of a fitness club. You received this email from the manager:

“Dear Member, I am sorry to inform you that the fitness centre is closing for a month from next Monday. The building needs some repairs and we also plan to install some new equipment. What else do you think should be changed? As the centre will not be operating for a month, what kind of alternative activities should we organise in the meantime? The Manager.”
Write an email to your friend, who is also a member of the club. Write about your feelings and what you think the club management should do about the situation.
content.note: Write about 50 words · ~7 minutes · Level B1 (informal email).
model: Hi John,

I’ve just read the email about our fitness club closing for a month. Honestly, I feel disappointed because I’ll miss my regular workouts. Still, I’m glad they’re adding new equipment. I think they should also improve the showers and expand the class schedule. Outdoor group sessions could be a great alternative during this break.
Best,
Mike

vocab.meaning: unhappy because something is not as good as you hoped
something you can do or choose instead of another
to make something bigger or wider

why: Answers both parts of the task — your feelings AND a clear suggestion for the club.
Uses the right informal shape: “Hi + name”, a warm opening line, and a short sign-off (“Best, Mike”).
Good B1 linking without over-writing: “Honestly”, “Still”, “also”.
Length and tone fit Task 1.1 — about 50 words, friendly, not a formal letter.

samples.title: Movie club renovation — email to a friend

task: You are a new member of a movie club. You received this email from the club:

“Dear Members, our theatre is now a bit outdated, so we have decided to do some renovations to make our weekly meetings more comfortable. We are going to change the seats and the sound equipment. Unfortunately, the club will be closed for a month during the renovation works.”
Write an email to your friend. Write about your feelings and what you think about the movie club plans.
content.note: Write about 50 words · ~7 minutes · Level B1 (informal email).
model: Hi Jack,

I’ve just received exciting news from the movie club! They are renovating the theatre with new seats and sound equipment. It will be closed for a month, but I think it’s a great idea. I’m really looking forward to enjoying films in a much more comfortable environment!
Best regards,
Kim

vocab.meaning: to repair and improve something, especially a building
to feel happy about something that is going to happen

why: Shows a clear, positive reaction and an opinion about the plans — both required.
Natural informal openers and closers (“Hi Jack”, “Best regards, Kim”).
“look forward to + -ing” is used correctly — a nice B1 structure.
Concise and on-topic at about 50 words.

samples.title: Cancelled food party — email to a friend

task: You are a member of a cooking club. You received this message from the club secretary:

“Dear member, I am writing to inform you that the International Food Party planned for tomorrow night will be cancelled, because we cannot use the kitchens at the local school as we had arranged. Unfortunately, we will not be able to offer a refund on the tickets you have purchased. The Club Secretary, Yum Yum Cooking Club.”
Write an email to your friend, who is also a member of the club, explaining your feelings and what you think the club should do about the situation. Use informal English.
content.note: Write 40–50 words · ~10 minutes · Level B1 (informal email).
model: Hey Sarah,

Did you hear the International Food Party got cancelled? I’m really disappointed — we were all looking forward to it! I think the club should try to find another place soon and maybe give us a discount for the next event. What do you think?
Talk later,
Emma

vocab.meaning: to decide that something planned will not happen
money that is given back to you
a reduction in the price of something

why: Gives feelings AND a practical suggestion (a new venue + a discount).
Very natural friendly tone: “Hey Sarah”, a dash for emphasis, and “What do you think?” to involve the reader.
Ends with a proper informal sign-off (“Talk later, Emma”).
Right length and register for an informal email to a friend.

samples.title: Fitness club closure — formal reply to the manager

task: You are a member of a fitness club. You received this email from the manager:

“Dear Member, I am sorry to inform you that the fitness centre is closing for a month from next Monday. The building needs some repairs and we also plan to install some new equipment. What else do you think should be changed? As the centre will not be operating for a month, what kind of alternative activities should we organise in the meantime? The Manager.”
Write an email to the manager. Write about your feelings and what you think the club management should do about the situation. Use a formal style.
content.note: Write 120–150 words · ~23 minutes · Level B2 (formal email).
model: Dear Sir,

Thank you for informing us about the temporary closure of the fitness club. Although I feel disappointed that I will not be able to train there for a whole month, I understand the need for repairs and I am pleased that new equipment will be installed.

In addition to the repairs, I believe it would be useful to upgrade the showers and lockers, as many members have mentioned they need improvement. It would also be great to expand the timetable with more group classes, such as yoga or spinning, as these activities are very popular among members.

During the closure, I suggest organising outdoor fitness sessions in local parks, which could be both fun and social. Online workout programmes would also be very helpful for members who prefer to exercise at home.
Yours sincerely,
[Your Name]

vocab.meaning: lasting for only a limited time
to improve something to a higher standard
a list of the times when classes or events happen

why: Fully formal from “Dear Sir” to “Yours sincerely” — no slips into chatty language.
Well organised: an intro paragraph (thanks + feelings), then suggestions grouped into paragraphs.
Polite, tentative language typical of B2: “I believe it would be useful…”, “I suggest…”.
Develops each idea with a reason (“as many members have mentioned…”) and stays within 120–150 words.

samples.title: Movie club renovation — formal reply to the president

task: You are a new member of a movie club. You received this email from the club:

“Dear Members, our theatre is now a bit outdated, so we have decided to do some renovations to make our weekly meetings more comfortable. We are going to change the seats and the sound equipment. Unfortunately, the club will be closed for a month during the renovation works.”
Write an email to the president of the club. Write about your feelings and what you think about the club plans. Use a formal style.
content.note: Write 120–150 words · ~23 minutes · Level B2 (formal email).
model: Dear President,

I have received your email regarding the renovation plans for our movie club, and I wanted to express my full support. I believe that updating the seats and sound system is an excellent idea, as it will definitely improve the overall movie-watching experience for all members.

Although it is unfortunate that the club will be closed for a month, I understand that this work is necessary, and I am sure it will be worth the wait. I would also like to offer my help as a volunteer during the renovation period. Please let me know how I can assist.
Thank you for your efforts in making the club a more enjoyable place for everyone. I am really looking forward to seeing the new improvements once the renovations are completed.
Best regards,
Mr. Michael

vocab.meaning: to state clearly that you agree with something
a person who offers to do something without being paid

why: Consistently formal register and a clear, supportive position stated at the start.
Three well-shaped paragraphs: reaction → understanding + offer of help → closing thanks.
Uses B2 phrases naturally: “regarding”, “express my full support”, “I would also like to offer…”.
Answers both parts (feelings + opinion on the plans) within the word limit.

samples.title: Cancelled food party — formal reply to the secretary

task: You are a member of a cooking club. You received this message from the club secretary:

“Dear member, I am writing to inform you that the International Food Party planned for tomorrow night will be cancelled, because we cannot use the kitchens at the local school as we had arranged. Unfortunately, we will not be able to offer a refund on the tickets you have purchased. The Club Secretary, Yum Yum Cooking Club.”
Now respond to the club secretary by writing a formal email. Include your feelings about the changes and what you think the club should do about the situation.
content.note: Write 120–150 words · ~20 minutes · Level B2 (formal email).
model: Dear Club Secretary,

I am writing regarding the cancellation of the International Food Party. While I understand the situation with the venue, I must say I am very disappointed, as many of us were looking forward to the event.

I believe it would be fair if the club could offer some form of compensation, perhaps a discount on future events or a small gift voucher. This would show appreciation for the members’ support and help maintain goodwill. Additionally, I would suggest the club consider finding an alternative venue, so that similar situations can be avoided in the future.
Thank you for informing us in advance. I look forward to hearing about the next event.
Kind regards,
[Your Full Name]

vocab.meaning: something given to make up for a loss or problem
a card or paper exchanged for goods or a discount
a friendly, helpful attitude towards others

why: Turns the same complaint into fully formal English: “I am writing regarding…”, “Kind regards”.
States feelings honestly, then makes concrete, reasonable requests (compensation, a new venue).
Good B2 hedging and linking: “While I understand…”, “perhaps”, “Additionally”.
Clear paragraphing and a polite close, within the 120–150 word range.

samples.title: Forum post: study alone or with a group?

task: You are participating in an online discussion for language learners. The question is:
“Is it better to study alone or with a group?”
Post your response, giving reasons and examples.
content.note: Write 180–200 words · ~30 minutes · Level C1 (forum post).
model: In my opinion, studying with a group is more effective than studying alone, especially for language learners. There are several reasons why I believe group study is better.

Firstly, studying with others helps you stay motivated. When you study alone, it is easy to lose focus or feel bored. However, in a group, you can share ideas, ask questions, and encourage each other. For example, if you don’t understand a grammar point, your group members can explain it to you in a simple way.

Secondly, group study allows you to practise speaking and listening skills, which are very important in language learning. You can do role plays, group discussions, or just have casual conversations. This is something you cannot do when you are studying alone.

Of course, some people prefer studying alone because they can concentrate better without distractions, and it allows them to study at their own pace. However, I think the benefits of studying with a group, such as interaction and motivation, are greater for improving communication skills.

vocab.meaning: having a strong reason or desire to do something
something that stops you paying attention
at a speed that is comfortable for you

why: Clear position in the introduction and defended all the way to the conclusion.
Well-signposted structure: “Firstly… Secondly… Of course… However…”.
Concedes the opposite view (studying alone) before answering it — a strong C1 move.
Each point is developed with a concrete example, and the length fits 180–200 words.

samples.title: Forum post: online courses or classroom lessons?

task: You are participating in an online discussion for language learners. The question is:
“Do you think learning a foreign language is more effective through online courses or traditional classroom lessons?”
Post your response, giving reasons and examples.
content.note: Write 180–200 words · ~30 minutes · Level C1 (forum post).
model: In my opinion, both online courses and traditional classroom lessons have their own advantages, but I believe online courses are becoming a more effective way to learn a foreign language. One of the main reasons is flexibility. Online platforms allow learners to study at their own pace and choose the time that suits their schedule, which is very convenient for people with busy lives. Also, online courses often provide a wide range of multimedia materials, such as videos, interactive exercises, and language apps that make learning more engaging and fun.

On the other hand, traditional classrooms offer direct interaction with teachers and classmates, which helps with speaking practice and immediate feedback. However, nowadays many online courses also include live sessions with tutors and virtual speaking clubs.

For example, I have been learning English through an online course, and I can practise listening, reading, and vocabulary at any time. I also join weekly speaking sessions with other learners. This method has helped me improve my skills faster than attending a class once or twice a week.
Overall, I believe online learning is more effective for people who are self-disciplined and motivated.

vocab.meaning: the ability to change easily to suit a situation
easy to use or suitable for your needs
able to control your own behaviour and keep working

why: Balanced answer that weighs both sides, then commits to a clear opinion.
Strong cohesion: “One of the main reasons…”, “On the other hand”, “However”, “Overall”.
Backs the argument with a convincing personal example.
Ends with a qualified conclusion (“for people who are self-disciplined”) rather than a bare repeat.

samples.title: Forum post: paper books or e-books?

task: You are participating in an online discussion for language learners. The question is:
“Do you think it’s better to read paper books or e-books? Why?”
Post your response, giving reasons and examples.
content.note: Write 180–200 words · ~30 minutes · Level C1 (forum post).
model: Personally, I prefer reading paper books because they give a special feeling that e-books cannot provide. Holding a real book and turning the pages makes reading more enjoyable and helps me concentrate better. I also find it easier on the eyes, as reading from a screen for too long can cause discomfort.

However, I understand why many people like e-books. They are very convenient because you can carry hundreds of books on one device. Also, e-books are often cheaper and offer features like built-in dictionaries, which help language learners quickly check the meanings of new words.

Despite these advantages, I still prefer paper books, especially when studying or reading for pleasure. I feel less distracted without notifications or screens, and I enjoy collecting books on my shelf. For example, when preparing for exams, I always choose printed textbooks because I can highlight important information and write notes on the pages.
In conclusion, while e-books are practical, I believe paper books create a better reading experience.

vocab.meaning: a feeling of being slightly uncomfortable or in slight pain
included as part of something, not added separately
without being affected by; in spite of

why: Takes a clear personal stance and keeps it consistent throughout.
Fairly presents the opposing view (e-books) before returning to its own position.
Uses concessive language well: “However”, “Despite these advantages”, “while e-books are practical”.
Supports claims with real, specific examples (highlighting textbooks, collecting books).

samples.title: Personal interview questions

task: In Part 1.1 the examiner asks short questions about you and your life. Answer naturally in a few sentences each — enough to show your range, but not a memorised speech.
content.note: 3–4 short questions · answer in 2–4 sentences each.

model.speaker: Examiner
model.text: Please tell me about your best friend.

model.speaker: Student
model.text: My best friend and I have been friends since we were in school. She is a kind and caring person who always has a smile on her face. We enjoy doing a lot of things together, like going shopping, watching movies, and exploring new places. She is always there for me when I need her, and I know I can count on her no matter what.

model.speaker: Examiner
model.text: Tell me about your country.

model.speaker: Student
model.text: Uzbekistan is a beautiful country located in Central Asia, known for its rich history, culture, and stunning architecture. The people are warm and hospitable, and the cuisine is delicious, with dishes like pilaf and kebabs being popular. The country is also home to the ancient Silk Road cities of Samarkand, Bukhara, and Khiva.

model.speaker: Examiner
model.text: What do you like to do in your free time?

model.speaker: Student
model.text: In my free time, I enjoy reading books, listening to music, and spending time outdoors. Reading allows me to explore new worlds and gain knowledge, while music helps me relax and unwind. Being outdoors, whether hiking or taking a walk, helps me appreciate nature and recharge for the week ahead.

vocab.meaning: to trust or rely on someone
friendly and welcoming to guests and visitors
to relax after a period of work

why: Each answer is extended but natural — a direct answer plus reasons and details.
Uses topic vocabulary precisely (“stunning”, “hospitable”, “count on”) instead of simple words.
Present perfect and relative clauses handled correctly (“have been friends since…”, “who always has a smile”).
Stays on topic and finishes each answer cleanly, without rambling.

samples.title: Comparing two pictures — driving vs walking

task: In Part 1.2 you are shown two pictures and asked to describe and compare them, then answer follow-up questions.
Questions: What do you see in these pictures? What are some advantages of walking over driving? Why do some people prefer having a car of their own?

images.alt: A man sitting behind the wheel of a car, focused on driving.
images.caption: Photo A

images.alt: A crowd of people crossing a busy city street on a pedestrian crossing.
images.caption: Photo B

content.note: Describe, compare, and give reasons — a few sentences per question.

model.speaker: Examiner
model.text: What do you see in these pictures?

model.speaker: Student
model.text: In the first picture, we see a man behind the wheel of his car, focused on driving. In the second picture, a crowd of people is crossing the road. These images highlight the importance of road safety and the need for mutual respect between drivers and pedestrians. It is crucial for drivers to pay attention to their surroundings and for pedestrians to use designated crosswalks.

model.speaker: Examiner
model.text: What are some advantages of walking over driving?

model.speaker: Student
model.text: Walking offers numerous advantages over driving. Firstly, it is cost-effective, as it does not require expenses for fuel or vehicle maintenance. Additionally, walking is environmentally friendly, producing zero emissions. It also promotes physical health by providing exercise and reducing the risk of obesity. Moreover, walking allows us to appreciate our surroundings and reduce stress.

model.speaker: Examiner
model.text: Why do some people prefer having a car of their own?

model.speaker: Student
model.text: Some people prefer having their own car for various reasons. A car provides convenience and flexibility, allowing them to travel at their own pace and schedule. It also offers privacy and comfort, especially during bad weather or long journeys. For people living in areas with limited public transport, a car can be essential.

vocab.meaning: a person who is walking, especially in a town
giving good value for the money spent
a gas or substance sent out into the air
the ability to change to suit a situation

why: Describes both pictures first, then compares — the order examiners expect.
Organises reasons with clear signposts: “Firstly”, “Additionally”, “Moreover”.
Uses precise topic vocabulary (pedestrians, emissions, cost-effective) accurately.
Answers every part of each question, including the follow-up on car ownership.

samples.title: A critical decision

task: In Part 2 you are shown a photo and asked to speak on a more personal or abstract topic, giving your own experience and opinion.
Questions: Tell me about a critical decision you have made. How has this decision influenced you and your life? What factors have the highest impact on the decisions people make?

images.alt: A person's feet in trainers standing on asphalt painted with white arrows pointing in many different directions.
images.caption: The prompt photo

content.note: Speak for about two minutes — a personal example plus a wider reflection.

model.speaker: Student
model.text: A critical decision that I made in my life was choosing to study abroad for a semester during my college years. This decision was influenced by my desire to experience a different culture and immerse myself in a new environment.

model.speaker: Student
model.text: This experience influenced me in many ways. Firstly, it helped me become more independent and self-reliant, as I navigated a new country on my own. I had to adapt to different customs, food, and language, which broadened my worldview. It also helped me develop valuable communication skills as I interacted with people from various backgrounds.

model.speaker: Student
model.text: When it comes to decision-making, I believe the factors with the highest impact are one’s values, goals, and priorities. It is important to align your decisions with what truly matters to you and to consider their long-term impact. Seeking advice from trusted mentors or friends can also provide valuable insights.

model.speaker: Student
model.text: In conclusion, critical decisions can have a profound impact on our lives and shape our personal and professional growth. It is essential to consider all factors carefully and to be willing to step outside our comfort zone.

vocab.meaning: to involve yourself deeply in an activity
not needing help or support from others
to widen the way you see the world
something that is very important and must be dealt with first
a situation where you feel comfortable and safe

why: Answers all three prompts in order: the decision, its effect, then a general reflection.
Develops a single personal example in depth instead of listing many shallow points.
Rich C1 vocabulary used naturally (“self-reliant”, “broadened my worldview”, “align”).
Rounds off with a genuine conclusion rather than stopping abruptly.

samples.title: For and against: personal gun ownership

task: In Part 3 you are given a statement with points FOR and AGAINST, and you discuss both sides before giving your own view. (Statement: “Citizens should be allowed to carry personal guns.”)

FOR: guns help people protect themselves; they deter crimes like burglary; they are needed for hunting or target sports. AGAINST: guns are used to commit crimes; fewer guns reduce the homicide rate; military-style guns are not useful for hunting.
content.note: Present both sides fairly, then reach a balanced conclusion.

model.speaker: Student
model.text: I would like to discuss whether citizens should be allowed to carry personal guns.

model.speaker: Student
model.text: On one hand, having a gun can help people protect themselves in dangerous situations. If someone is being threatened, a gun can give them a sense of security. Guns can also deter criminals from committing crimes like burglary, as they may think twice before targeting someone who is armed. In addition, many people use guns for recreational activities such as hunting or target sports.

model.speaker: Student
model.text: On the other hand, guns are often associated with crime and violence. They can be misused to commit crimes, which is a serious concern. Some argue that reducing the number of guns in society could help lower the homicide rate. Furthermore, military-style weapons are not necessary for activities like hunting and may pose a greater risk if they fall into the wrong hands.

model.speaker: Student
model.text: In conclusion, the debate is complicated. While guns can offer protection and have valid uses, they also pose real risks. I believe it is important to strike a balance between individual rights and public safety when deciding on gun ownership laws.

vocab.meaning: to make someone decide not to do something
the crime of entering a building to steal
the crime of killing a person; murder
to use something in a wrong or harmful way
to give fair attention to two opposing things

why: Presents FOR and AGAINST in separate, balanced paragraphs — exactly the Part 3 task.
Uses the discourse markers examiners reward: “On one hand”, “On the other hand”, “Furthermore”, “In conclusion”.
Reaches a measured personal conclusion instead of taking an extreme side.
Precise topic vocabulary (deter, homicide, strike a balance) used correctly.

samples.title: What would it be like living in these two places?

task: In Part 1.2 you are shown two pictures and asked to describe and compare them, then answer follow-up questions.
Questions: Tell me what you see in the two pictures What would it be like living in these two places? Which of these two places would it be better for old people to live in?

images.alt: Two pictures to describe and compare
images.caption: The two pictures

content.note: Describe, compare, and give reasons, a few sentences per question.

model.speaker: Examiner
model.text: Tell me what you see in the two pictures

model.speaker: Student
model.text: In the first picture, I see tall buildings that are part of a busy city. The buildings are close together, and the area looks lively with lots of activity. In the second picture, the scene changes to a calm, peaceful village. There are small houses and open spaces, with a quiet and relaxed atmosphere. The village feels serene and less crowded compared to the busy city.

model.speaker: Examiner
model.text: What would it be like living in these two places?

model.speaker: Student
model.text: Living in a bustling city with tall buildings means you're surrounded by excitement and lots of opportunities. There are always things to do and people to meet, but it can also be noisy and busy. On the other hand, living in a peaceful village is much quieter and slower-paced. You can enjoy nature and a close-knit community, but there might be fewer activities and amenities nearby. Both have their own charm: the city offers energy and convenience, while the village provides calm and a sense of tranquility.

model.speaker: Examiner
model.text: Which of these two places would it be better for old people to live in?

model.speaker: Student
model.text: For older people, a peaceful village might be a better place to live. The calm environment is gentle on the senses and provides a relaxing atmosphere. There are fewer loud noises and less stress, which can be comforting. The village often has a close-knit community, making it easier to form friendships and get support. However, it's important to consider access to medical services and other needs. In contrast, a city offers more services and activities, but the hustle and bustle can be overwhelming. So, a quiet village is often a better choice for a serene and supportive living environment.

samples.title: Tell me about the movie you enjoyed watching is going to cinema a…

Questions: Tell me about the movie you enjoyed watching is going to cinema a popular activity in your country? What people can learn from watching movies?

images.alt: Photo for the Part 2 talk
images.caption: The photo

samples.title: Social media and its impact on teenagers

task: In Part 3 you are given a statement with points for and against; discuss both sides, then give your own view. The prompt is shown below.

images.alt: The Part 3 statement, with points for and against
images.caption: The prompt

content.note: Present both sides fairly, then reach a balanced conclusion.

model.speaker: Student
model.text: Today, I want to talk about social media and its impact on teenagers. There are both good and bad aspects to consider.

model.speaker: Student
model.text: Let's start with the concerns. First, social media can lead to anxiety, depression, and low self-esteem. When teens see others posting perfect pictures or achievements, they might feel like they don't measure up. Second, there's the risk of cyberbullying. This is when someone uses the Internet to hurt or threaten others, which can have serious emotional effects. Third, social media can be addictive. Teens might spend so much time online that they neglect their studies and other important activities.

model.speaker: Student
model.text: However, social media also has some positive sides. It helps teenagers stay connected with friends and family, which is especially useful when they can't be together in person. It also provides a platform for self-expression, allowing teens to share their interests and passions. Plus, social media can offer access to educational resources, helping teens learn new things and enhance their knowledge.

model.speaker: Student
model.text: In summary, while there are valid concerns about social media's impact on teenagers, it also offers valuable benefits. The key is to use it wisely and maintain a healthy balance between online and offline activities.

samples.title: What kind of music do you like and why?

task: In Part 1.1 the examiner asks short questions about you and your life. Answer naturally in a few sentences each, enough to show your range but not a memorised speech.
content.note: 3-4 short questions · answer in 2-4 sentences each.

model.speaker: Examiner
model.text: What kind of music do you like and why?

samples.title: Increasing fines for littering is a topic that has both benefits and…

task: In Part 3 you are given a statement with points for and against; discuss both sides, then give your own view. The prompt is shown below.

images.alt: The Part 3 statement, with points for and against
images.caption: The prompt

content.note: Present both sides fairly, then reach a balanced conclusion.

model.speaker: Student
model.text: Increasing fines for littering is a topic that has both benefits and challenges.

model.speaker: Student
model.text: On the positive side, higher fines can be a strong deterrent. When people know they could face a hefty fine for littering, they might think twice before throwing trash on the ground. This can help keep public spaces cleaner and safer for everyone, making parks, streets, and other areas more enjoyable to use. Additionally, the money collected from fines could be used to fund more cleanup efforts, improving our environment further.

model.speaker: Student
model.text: However, there are some downsides to consider. Higher fines might seem unfair to those who litter by accident. For example, someone might accidentally drop a piece of trash without realizing it, and a high fine could be a harsh penalty for a small mistake. Enforcing and collecting these fines could also be challenging. It might require more resources and effort from local authorities to monitor and manage littering violations effectively.

model.speaker: Student
model.text: Moreover, increasing fines alone might not address the root causes of littering. Some people litter because they lack access to proper disposal options or may not understand the environmental impact of their actions. Therefore, while higher fines can help, they should be part of a broader strategy that includes education and improved waste management facilities.

model.speaker: Student
model.text: In summary, while raising fines for littering could help keep public spaces clean and fund cleanup efforts, it's important to balance this with fairness and practical enforcement. Combining stricter fines with education and better waste management could be the most effective way to address littering and promote a cleaner, more responsible society.

model.speaker: Student

model.speaker: Student
model.text: Today, I want to talk about whether smartphones should be banned in schools.

model.speaker: Student
model.text: On one side, there are good reasons to ban them. First, smartphones can distract students from learning, as they might scroll through social media or play games instead of focusing. Second, banning phones can encourage better face-to-face interactions, helping students build social skills and friendships. Lastly, smartphones can lead to bullying, as they make it easier to harass others online.

model.speaker: Student
model.text: On the other hand, there are strong reasons against a ban. Smartphones can be useful educational tools. Students can use them for apps, online research, and other resources that support learning. Having a smartphone also allows students to quickly contact their parents in emergencies, ensuring their safety. Plus, using smartphones in school helps students learn important technology skills for the future.

model.speaker: Student
model.text: In conclusion, while there are real concerns about distractions and bullying, we should also consider the educational benefits and safety that smartphones provide. A balanced approach may be the best way to use their advantages while reducing their downsides. Thank you.

samples.title: Adopting a four-week work schedule could bring significant benefits

images.caption: The prompt

content.note: Present both sides fairly, then reach a balanced conclusion.

model.speaker: Student
model.text: Adopting a four-week work schedule could bring significant benefits. It would offer employees a better work-life balance with more time for family, relaxation, or hobbies, leading to reduced burnout and improved well-being. This extra time off could also boost productivity during working hours, as employees may focus more and work efficiently with the prospect of a longer break. Additionally, reducing commuting days would lower traffic, pollution, and commuting time, benefiting the environment.

model.speaker: Student
model.text: However, there are challenges. A shorter workweek might reduce overall economic output, particularly in industries with tight deadlines or continuous operations. Employers could also face higher costs by needing to hire more staff or offer higher pay to meet demands. Finally, in some sectors, service disruptions or inefficiencies could arise from fewer working days, affecting customer satisfaction.

model.speaker: Student
model.text: In conclusion, while a four-week work schedule has clear benefits, its implementation would need to be carefully considered to balance the advantages with the potential drawbacks.
